channel.title: Security on Sepe Gennaro
channel.description: Recent content in Security on Sepe Gennaro

item.title: XKeyscore
item.description: The XKeyscore program is one of the most pervasive and advanced pieces of software in the mass surveillance apparatus operated by the National Security Agency (NSA). In various classified documents and training materials, this tool is described as the cornerstone for collecting and analyzing information from computer networks, a process known in technical jargon as Digital Network Intelligence. Its most striking feature is its immense capacity to monitor global digital activity, covering nearly everything a user does on the internet. Through a simple interface, which mimics the operation of a standard web search engine, intelligence analysts can search numerous databases without needing a court order or prior review by superiors or courts. To launch a search, one simply enters a general justification into an online form, gaining immediate access to historical data and real-time intercepts. This complete absence of technical or legal safeguards within the software merely serves to confirm the validity of the controversial statements made in 2013 by the whistleblower Edward Snowden, who claimed that, from his desk as a private consultant for the NSA, he could have electronically intercepted anyone, from ordinary citizens to federal judges right up to the President of the United States, provided they had a simple email address.

item.title: FAST16
item.description:
SentinelOne recently published an in-depth analysis describing the discovery of a new cyber-sabotage framework called fast16, which forces us to reconsider the timeline of the development of cyber operations carried out by state-sponsored actors in the first half of the 2000s.

In particular, this malware, written in Lua and dating back to 2005, predates the infamous Stuxnet by at least five years – the so-called first digital weapon known to have physically targeted Iran’s nuclear infrastructure.
